old_value: {"id":"smallhold-fihu.11","title":"HIGH: CORS allows any origin with full access","description":"In src/server.rs lines 14-18, the CORS layer is configured with allow_origin(Any), allow_methods(Any), allow_headers(Any), expose_headers(Any). This means any website can make credentialed cross-origin requests to the API (including Authorization headers) and read the responses. While the Mastodon API does need somewhat permissive CORS (for browser-based clients), allow_origin(Any) combined with allow_headers(Any) allows any malicious website visited by the admin to silently make authenticated API calls if the browser has cached credentials or if a token is known. The Mastodon specification expects CORS to be permissive for public endpoints but more restrictive for authenticated ones. At minimum, consider not exposing Authorization in allowed headers from arbitrary origins, or use a configured allowlist of client origins.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"mark@wolfssl.com","created_at":"2026-07-20T01:13:19.803834648Z","created_by":"Mark Atwood","updated_at":"2026-07-20T01:13:19.803834648Z","labels":["security"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"smallhold-fihu.11","depends_on_id":"smallhold-fihu","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-07-20T01:13:19.832492621Z","created_by":"Mark Atwood","metadata":"{}"}]}
new_value: {"description":"In src/server.rs lines 14-18, the CORS layer is configured with allow_origin(Any), allow_methods(Any), allow_headers(Any), expose_headers(Any). This is standard for Mastodon-compatible APIs (Mastodon itself uses Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *) since browser clients like Elk and Pinafore run on different origins and use Bearer tokens. With wildcard origin, browsers do not send credentials (cookies), so the risk is limited to scenarios where a malicious site already possesses a valid Bearer token. However, expose_headers(Any) is more permissive than needed -- consider restricting to the specific headers clients need (Link, X-RateLimit-*, etc.).","priority":3}

This is standard for Mastodon-compatible APIs (Mastodon itself uses Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *) since browser clients like Elk and Pinafore run on different origins and use Bearer tokens. With wildcard origin, browsers do not send credentials (cookies), so the risk is limited to scenarios where a malicious site already possesses a valid Bearer token. However, expose_headers(Any) is more permissive than needed -- consider restricting to the specific headers clients need (Link, X-RateLimit-*, etc.).","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"mark@wolfssl.com","created_at":"2026-07-20T01:13:19.803834648Z","created_by":"Mark Atwood","updated_at":"2026-07-20T01:13:35.272629267Z","labels":["security"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"smallhold-fihu.11","depends_on_id":"smallhold-fihu","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-07-20T01:13:19.832492621Z","created_by":"Mark Atwood","metadata":"{}"}]}

new_value: By design: Mastodon Client API requires permissive CORS for browser-based clients (Elk, Phanpy, Pinafore)
